content:
# Luke Chapter 6 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 91-93

---

**1** One Sabbath, Jesus was passing through some grain fields. His disciples were picking heads of grain, rubbing them in their hands, and eating them. **2** Some of the Pharisees said, "Why are you doing what is not permitted on the Sabbath?"

**3** Jesus answered them, "Have you never read what David did when he and his companions were hungry? **4** He entered the house of God, took the sacred bread of offering, ate it, and gave some to those with him — bread that only the priests are allowed to eat."

**5** Then he said to them, "The Son of Man is lord of the Sabbath."

**6** On another Sabbath he went into the synagogue and was teaching. A man was there whose right hand was withered. **7** The scribes and Pharisees were watching him closely to see whether he would heal on the Sabbath, so they could find a charge against him. **8** But he knew their thoughts. He said to the man with the withered hand, "Get up and stand in the middle." The man got up and stood there.

**9** Then Jesus said to them, "I ask you: is it lawful on the Sabbath to do good or to do harm — to save a life or to destroy one?"

**10** He looked around at all of them and said to the man, "Stretch out your hand." He did, and his hand was completely restored. **11** But they were filled with fury and began discussing with one another what they might do to Jesus.

**12** During those days he went out to the mountain to pray, and he spent the entire night in prayer to God. **13** When daylight came, he called his disciples to himself and chose twelve of them, whom he also named apostles: **14** Simon, whom he also named Peter, and his brother Andrew; James and John; Philip and Bartholomew; **15** Matthew and Thomas; James the son of Alphaeus and Simon who was called the Zealot; **16** Judas the son of James, and Judas Iscariot, who became the traitor.

**17** He came down with them and stood on a level place. A large crowd of his disciples was there, along with a great multitude of people from all of Judea, from Jerusalem, and from the coastal region of Tyre and Sidon. **18** They had come to hear him and to be healed of their diseases. Those tormented by unclean spirits were being cured, **19** and the whole crowd was trying to touch him, because power was flowing out of him and healing them all.

**20** Then he looked up at his disciples and said:

"Blessed are you who are poor,
because the kingdom of God is yours.

**21** Blessed are you who hunger now,
because you will be filled.

Blessed are you who weep now,
because you will laugh.

**22** Blessed are you when people hate you, when they exclude you and insult you and reject your name as evil because of the Son of Man.

**23** Rejoice on that day and leap for joy! Your reward in heaven is great. Their ancestors treated the prophets the same way.

**24** But how terrible for you who are rich,
because you have already received your comfort.

**25** How terrible for you who are full now,
because you will go hungry.

How terrible for you who laugh now,
because you will mourn and weep.

**26** How terrible for you when everyone speaks well of you — their ancestors treated the false prophets the same way.

**27** "But I say to you who are listening: Love your enemies. Do good to those who hate you. **28** Bless those who curse you. Pray for those who mistreat you.

**29** If someone strikes you on the cheek, offer the other one as well. If someone takes your cloak, do not withhold your tunic either. **30** Give to everyone who asks you, and if someone takes what is yours, do not demand it back. **31** Treat others the way you want them to treat you.

**32** "If you love only those who love you, what credit is that to you? Even sinners love those who love them. **33** If you do good only to those who do good to you, what credit is that to you? Even sinners do the same. **34** If you lend only to those from whom you expect repayment, what credit is that to you? Even sinners lend to sinners, expecting to be repaid in full.

**35** "No — love your enemies and do good and lend without expecting anything back. Then your reward will be great, and you will be children of the Most High, because he is kind to the ungrateful and the wicked. **36** Be compassionate, just as your Father is compassionate.

**37** "Do not judge, and you will not be judged. Do not condemn, and you will not be condemned. Forgive, and you will be forgiven. **38** Give, and it will be given to you — a generous portion, pressed down, shaken together, and overflowing, will be poured into your lap. The measure you use for others is the measure that will be used for you."

**39** He also told them a parable: "Can a blind person lead a blind person? Will they not both fall into a pit? **40** A student is not above the teacher; but everyone who is fully trained will be like their teacher.

**41** "Why do you notice the splinter in your brother's eye but fail to see the plank in your own? **42** How can you say to your brother, 'Brother, let me take the splinter out of your eye,' when you cannot even see the plank in your own eye? You hypocrite! First remove the plank from your own eye, and then you will see clearly enough to remove the splinter from your brother's eye.

**43** "No good tree produces rotten fruit, and no rotten tree produces good fruit. **44** Each tree is recognized by its own fruit. People do not pick figs from thorn bushes, or harvest grapes from a bramble bush. **45** A good person brings good things out of the good stored up in their heart, and an evil person brings evil things out of the evil stored up in theirs. For the mouth speaks from whatever fills the heart.

**46** "Why do you call me 'Lord, Lord,' but do not do what I say?

**47** "Everyone who comes to me and hears my words and acts on them — I will show you what that person is like. **48** They are like someone building a house who dug deep and laid the foundation on bedrock. When a flood came, the river crashed against that house but could not shake it, because it was well built. **49** But the one who hears and does not act is like someone who built a house on the ground without a foundation. The moment the river struck it, the house collapsed — and the destruction of that house was total."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

1. **6:1 — "Rubbing them in their hands"**: Sinaiticus includes *psochontes* ("rubbing"), a detail that explains why the Pharisees objected — rubbing grain in the hands could be classified as threshing, which was prohibited work on the Sabbath. The issue was not picking grain (permitted for the hungry under Deuteronomy 23:25) but the manner of preparation.

2. **6:5 — "Lord of the Sabbath"**: The Sinaiticus text reads *kyrios estin tou sabbatou ho huios tou anthropou*. This is a sweeping claim of authority: the Son of Man stands above the Sabbath institution itself. In the dynamic rendering, placing this as a standalone statement after the David argument lets the weight of the claim land with full force.

3. **6:6 — "Right hand"**: Luke the physician specifies it was the *right* hand (*dexia*) — a clinical detail absent from Mark's parallel account. The right hand was the hand of action and livelihood, making the disability both practical and socially devastating.

4. **6:9 — "Save a life or destroy one"**: Jesus reframes the question entirely. The Pharisees asked whether healing was lawful on the Sabbath; Jesus asks whether it is lawful to do good or do harm. Inaction in the face of suffering becomes itself an act of destruction. This rhetorical move is characteristic of Jesus' Sabbath arguments throughout Luke.

5. **6:12 — "Spent the entire night in prayer"**: The Greek *en dienuktereuen en te proseuchhe tou theou* — literally "was spending the night in the prayer of God." This is the most intensive description of Jesus' prayer life in the Gospels. The selection of the Twelve follows directly from this night of communion with the Father, linking apostolic calling to divine initiative.

6. **6:15 — "The Zealot" (zeloten)**: Sinaiticus reads *zeloten*. This likely designates Simon's political orientation — associated with the movement that advocated armed resistance to Rome. Jesus' inner circle thus included both a tax collector (Matthew, who collaborated with Rome) and a Zealot (who opposed it). The kingdom Jesus proclaimed transcended political alignments.

7. **6:17 — "A level place"**: The Greek *epi topou pedinou* — "on a level place." Unlike Matthew's Sermon on the Mount, Luke places this teaching on flat ground after coming down from the mountain. Whether this describes the same occasion differently or a separate event, Luke's "level place" may carry symbolic freight: Jesus brings his teaching down to where the people are.

8. **6:20 — "Blessed are you who are poor"**: Unlike Matthew's "poor in spirit," Sinaiticus Luke reads simply *hoi ptochoi* — "the poor." Luke's version is direct and material. Throughout his Gospel, Luke emphasizes God's particular concern for the economically destitute. The second person ("you") makes this even more immediate — Jesus is speaking directly to real poor people standing before him.

9. **6:24-26 — The Woes**: These four woes, found only in Luke, form a perfect counterpoint to the four Beatitudes. "How terrible" renders *ouai*, which expresses grief and lament rather than vindictive threat. Jesus is not cursing the rich but mourning the spiritual danger of their condition.

10. **6:29 — "Offer the other cheek"**: A strike on the right cheek (implied by the culture) would be a backhanded blow — an act of insult and dominance rather than a fistfight. Offering the other cheek is not passive submission but a refusal to be degraded. It forces the aggressor to confront the full humanity of the one being struck.

11. **6:31 — The Golden Rule**: The Sinaiticus form reads *kathos thelete hina poiosin humin hoi anthropoi, poieite autois homoios* — "As you want people to do to you, do likewise to them." Luke places this principle within the context of enemy love (vv. 27-36), giving it a far more radical scope than it carries in isolation. It is not merely about mutual courtesy but about extending generosity to those who would harm you.

12. **6:35 — "Children of the Most High"**: The Greek *huioi hupsistou* — "sons of the Most High." The promise is astonishing: those who love their enemies share in the very character of God. "Children" captures the relational and identity-forming dimension of *huioi* in dynamic English.

13. **6:36 — "Compassionate" (oiktirmones)**: Where Matthew's parallel reads "perfect" (*teleioi*), Luke reads *oiktirmones* — "compassionate" or "merciful." This single word sums up Luke's portrait of the Father: not abstract perfection but active, feeling compassion. This is the standard by which Jesus' followers are measured.

14. **6:38 — "Poured into your lap"**: The image is drawn from the marketplace. The *kolpos* ("lap" or "bosom") refers to the fold of a garment used as a large pocket or pouch. The picture is of grain being measured out with extravagant generosity — pressed down, shaken to settle, and still overflowing — then poured into the fold of your robe. God's generosity exceeds all human calculation.

15. **6:48-49 — The Two Foundations**: Sinaiticus reads that the wise builder "dug deep" (*eskapsen kai ebathunen*) and laid the foundation on rock (*petra*). The foolish builder built "on the ground without a foundation" (*epi ten gen choris themeliou*). Luke's version emphasizes the effort and intentionality required — one must dig deep. The destruction of the poorly built house is described with a word (*rhegma*) that suggests a sudden, catastrophic collapse. "The destruction of that house was total" captures the finality of *mega to rhegma*.
